---
title: OpenSNP Analysis
output:
  html_document:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 2
    css: styles/styles.css
    includes:
      in_header: header.html
      after_body: footer.html

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)
```

***

A new upload of the OpenSNP data is now available on Zenodo: https://zenodo.org/records/18493898

OpenSNP aggregates direct-to-consumer genotyping data uploaded by users of
23andMe, AncestryDNA and FamilyTreeDNA. Within each company the uploads come
from a mix of chip generations (e.g. 23andMe v3 OmniExpress+, v4
OmniExpress+ custom, v5 GSA), so concatenating everything into a single
PLINK fileset produces extremely patchy variant-level missingness — the
union of the chips is much larger than what any single sample carries.

To make the data usable for imputation and downstream polygenic scoring we
need to **separate each company's data into per-chip filesets** before
imputing. The strategy used throughout this document is:

1. Run `plink2 --missing` on the raw fileset and look at the histogram of
   per-sample non-missing variant counts (`NONMISSING_CT`).
2. If samples form clear clusters in `NONMISSING_CT`, split on those
   thresholds.
3. If samples cluster only weakly (because two chips have similar SNP
   counts), restrict to the *high-peak* variants — those with intermediate
   variant-level `F_MISS`, i.e. variants present in only a subset of
   samples — and re-compute sample missingness on that subset. The clusters
   are usually obvious there.
4. Once per-chip `.keep` files are written, run
   `--make-bed` → `--geno 0.05` → `--mind 0.05` to drop variants and
   samples that don't fit cleanly.

The final cross-array variant-overlap check at the bottom of this document
is what tells us whether each split actually captured a different chip, or
just two density tiers of the same chip.

***

# Download and pre-process

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
mkdir -p ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898

cd ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898

wget https://zenodo.org/records/18493898/files/23andme.zip?download=1
wget https://zenodo.org/records/18493898/files/AncestryDNA.zip?download=1
wget https://zenodo.org/records/18493898/files/FamilyTreeDNA.zip?download=1

mv '23andme.zip?download=1' 23andme.zip
mv 'AncestryDNA.zip?download=1' AncestryDNA.zip
mv 'FamilyTreeDNA.zip?download=1' FamilyTreeDNA.zip

unzip 23andme.zip
unzip AncestryDNA.zip
unzip FamilyTreeDNA.zip

```

</details>

Each archive ships both an unfiltered and a `_filtered` fileset (the
`_filtered` one was produced by the OpenSNP curators and intersects
variants across chips, hence the much smaller variant counts). Inspect
both:

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
library(data.table)
# Check sample size and number of variants in filtered and unfiltered versions of each dataset
datasets <- c('23andme','AncestryDNA','FamilyTreeDNA')
types <- c('filtered','unfiltered')

stats <- NULL
for(i in datasets){
  for(j in types){
    tmp_bim <- fread(
      paste0(
        '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/',
        i,
        ifelse(j == 'filtered', '_filtered', ''),
        '.bim'
      ),
      header = F
    )
    tmp_fam <- fread(
      paste0(
        '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/',
        i,
        ifelse(j == 'filtered', '_filtered', ''),
        '.fam'
      ),
      header = F
    )
    stats <- rbind(
      stats,
      data.frame(
        dataset = i,
        type = j,
        n_var = nrow(tmp_bim),
        n_ind = nrow(tmp_fam)
      )
    )
  }
}

```

</details>

Compute per-sample and per-variant missingness for every company's
unfiltered fileset — this is the input to the per-company splitting work
below:

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
# Compute per-sample (.smiss) and per-variant (.vmiss) missingness on the
# unfiltered, all-chip fileset for each company.

for dataset in $(echo 23andme AncestryDNA FamilyTreeDNA); do
  /users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
    --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/${dataset} \
    --missing \
    --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/${dataset}
done
```

</details>

***

## 23andMe

23andMe has used three chip generations (per their public documentation):

| Chip | Supplier | Chip name | SNPs |
|------|----------|-----------|------|
| v3 | Illumina | OmniExpress+ | ~967,000 |
| v4 | Illumina | OmniExpress+ (custom) | ~570,000 |
| v5 | Illumina | GSA | ~640,000 (+60k custom) |

### Step 1 — split v3 from v4+v5 by sample-level NONMISSING_CT

The per-sample `NONMISSING_CT` histogram shows a clear bimodal pattern:
samples genotyped on the v3 (OmniExpress+) chip carry ~900k+ variants,
while v4 and v5 samples carry far fewer. A single threshold at 900,000
cleanly separates v3 from the v4+v5 mix.

<details><summary>Show plot — 23andMe per-sample NONMISSING_CT (all variants)</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/23andme_smiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
smiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme.smiss')
smiss$NONMISSING_CT <- smiss$OBS_CT - smiss$MISSING_CT
hist(smiss$NONMISSING_CT, 20)

# Samples with > 900k non-missing variants are the v3 (OmniExpress+) chip.
dat_v3 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT > 900000]

write.table(
  cbind(dat_v3, dat_v3),
  '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_more_900k.keep',
  row.names = F,
  col.names = F,
  quote = F
)

```

</details>

Re-run `--missing` on each side of the 900k split so we can investigate
the v4-vs-v5 structure on its own:

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
# Compute missingness within each side of the v3 / not-v3 split.

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme \
  --keep ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_more_900k.keep \
  --missing \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_more_900k

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme \
  --remove ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_more_900k.keep \
  --missing \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_less_900k

```

</details>

### Step 2 — separate v4 and v5 within the <900k group

v4 and v5 carry similar numbers of variants, so the sample-level histogram
isn't enough to split them. Instead, inspect the **variant-level** F_MISS
distribution within the <900k samples: the high-F_MISS peak corresponds to
variants present on only one of the two chips. The chunk below cross-checks
those high-peak variant IDs against the GSA-24v1 and OmniExpress-24
manifests and confirms they are GSA-array variants.

<details><summary>Show plot — 23andMe (<900k samples) per-variant F_MISS</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/23andme_less_900k_vmiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
vmiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_less_900k.vmiss')
vmiss <- vmiss[vmiss$F_MISS < 0.8, ]
hist(vmiss$F_MISS, 100)

# Variants with F_MISS > 0.55 are present in a minority of <900k samples.
# These are the discriminating set between v4 and v5.
vmiss_high_peak <- vmiss$ID[vmiss$F_MISS > 0.55]
write.table(
  vmiss_high_peak,
  '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_high_peak.extract',
  row.names = F,
  col.names = F,
  quote = F)

# Cross-check the high-peak variants against the chip manifests:
gsa <- fread(cmd = "cut -f 1 -d',' ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/GSA-24v1-0_C1.csv | tail -n +8", header =T)
gsa <- gsa[grepl('^GSA-rs', gsa$IlmnID),]
gsa$rsid<-gsub('-.*','', gsub('GSA-','',gsa$IlmnID))

omni <- fread(cmd = "cut -f 1 -d',' ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/humanomniexpress-24-v1-1-a.csv | tail -n +8", header =T)
omni <- omni[grepl('^rs', omni$IlmnID),]
omni$rsid<-gsub('-.*','',omni$IlmnID)

sum(vmiss_high_peak %in% gsa$rsid)
sum(vmiss_high_peak %in% omni$rsid)
# The GSA hit count is much higher than OmniExpress -> high-peak variants
# are GSA-array variants -> samples carrying them are v5.
```

</details>

Recompute per-sample missingness within the <900k samples but restricted to
those high-peak (GSA) variants:

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme \
  --remove ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_more_900k.keep \
  --extract ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_high_peak.extract \
  --missing \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_less_900k_high_peak

```

</details>

The resulting `NONMISSING_CT` histogram is cleanly bimodal: samples that
carry many of the GSA-specific variants (>200k) are v5; the rest are v4.

<details><summary>Show plot — 23andMe NONMISSING_CT within high-peak (GSA) variants</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/23andme_less_900k_high_peak_smiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
smiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_less_900k_high_peak.smiss')
smiss$NONMISSING_CT <- smiss$OBS_CT - smiss$MISSING_CT
hist(smiss$NONMISSING_CT, 20)

dat_v5 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT > 200000]
dat_v4 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT < 200000]

write.table(
  cbind(dat_v5, dat_v5),
  '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v5.keep',
  row.names = F,
  col.names = F,
  quote = F
)

write.table(
  cbind(dat_v4, dat_v4),
  '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v4.keep',
  row.names = F,
  col.names = F,
  quote = F
)

```

</details>

Sanity-check the v4 split by re-running `--missing` on it alone — both the
sample and variant histograms should be tight (single chip):

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme \
  --keep ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v4.keep \
  --missing \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v4

```

```{r}
smiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v4.smiss')
smiss$NONMISSING_CT <- smiss$OBS_CT - smiss$MISSING_CT
hist(smiss$NONMISSING_CT, 20)

vmiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v4.vmiss')
vmiss <- vmiss[vmiss$F_MISS < 0.8, ]
hist(vmiss$F_MISS, 100)
# Single tight peak at high NONMISSING_CT and at low F_MISS = clean v4 chip.
```

</details>

### Step 3 — make per-array filesets and apply --geno / --mind

Materialise the v3, v4, v5 keep files as PLINK filesets, then apply
`--geno 0.05` (drop variants missing in >5% of samples *within that chip*)
and `--mind 0.05` (drop the few samples that are still patchy after
splitting).

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme \
  --keep ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_more_900k.keep \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v3

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme \
  --keep ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v4.keep \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v4

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme \
  --keep ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v5.keep \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_v5

for version in $(echo v3 v4 v5); do
/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_${version} \
  --geno 0.05 \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_${version}_geno

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_${version}_geno \
  --mind 0.05 \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/23andme_${version}_filtered
done

```

</details>

***

## AncestryDNA

AncestryDNA's sample-level `NONMISSING_CT` histogram is unimodal at the
top level, even though multiple chips are present in the data. The
split-by-sample-missingness trick that worked for 23andMe doesn't apply
directly here. Instead we use the variant-level `F_MISS` distribution to
identify "high-peak" variants — variants present in only a subset of
samples — and use those as a probe to separate one chip at a time.

### Step 1 — initial inspection

The per-sample histogram has a long thin lower tail but a single dominant
mode around 660–680k variants. The per-variant `F_MISS` histogram, by
contrast, has multiple bands — characteristic of a mixture of chips
sharing a common backbone.

<details><summary>Show plot — AncestryDNA per-sample NONMISSING_CT</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/AncestryDNA_smiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show plot — AncestryDNA per-variant F_MISS</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/AncestryDNA_vmiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
smiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA.smiss')
smiss$NONMISSING_CT <- smiss$OBS_CT - smiss$MISSING_CT
hist(smiss$NONMISSING_CT, 50,
     main = 'AncestryDNA: per-sample non-missing count (all variants)',
     xlab = 'NONMISSING_CT')

vmiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA.vmiss')
vmiss <- vmiss[vmiss$F_MISS < 0.8, ]
hist(vmiss$F_MISS, 50,
     main = 'AncestryDNA: per-variant F_MISS (clipped <0.8)',
     xlab = 'F_MISS')
abline(v = 0.5, col = 'red', lty = 2)
legend('topright', 'high-peak threshold = 0.5', col = 'red', lty = 2, bty = 'n')

# Variants with F_MISS > 0.5 are present in a minority of samples ->
# carrier samples should cluster apart in the next step.
vmiss_high_peak <- vmiss$ID[vmiss$F_MISS > 0.5]
write.table(
  vmiss_high_peak,
  '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_high_peak.extract',
  row.names = F,
  col.names = F,
  quote = F)
```

</details>

### Step 2 — split v1 using high-peak variants

Recompute sample missingness restricted to those high-peak variants:

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA \
  --extract ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_high_peak.extract \
  --missing \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_high_peak

```

</details>

The resulting histogram is cleanly bimodal — ~331 samples carry most of
the high-peak variants (NONMISSING_CT > 200,000) and form **v1**, while
the rest don't carry them and need further splitting.

<details><summary>Show plot — AncestryDNA NONMISSING_CT within high-peak variants</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/AncestryDNA_high_peak_smiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
smiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_high_peak.smiss')
smiss$NONMISSING_CT <- smiss$OBS_CT - smiss$MISSING_CT
hist(smiss$NONMISSING_CT, 50,
     main = 'AncestryDNA: per-sample NONMISSING_CT within high-peak variants\n(splits v1 from rest)',
     xlab = 'NONMISSING_CT')
abline(v = 200000, col = 'red', lty = 2)
legend('top', 'v1 threshold = 200,000', col = 'red', lty = 2, bty = 'n')

# Samples carrying >200k high-peak variants are the v1 chip.
keep <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT > 200000]
write.table(
  cbind(keep, keep),
  '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_v1.keep',
  row.names = F,
  col.names = F,
  quote = F
)

```

</details>

### Step 3 — recurse on the not-v1 group

Compute missingness on the not-v1 samples to see whether the remaining
~806 samples are a single chip or a further mixture:

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA \
  --remove ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_v1.keep \
  --missing \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_not_v1

```

</details>

The not-v1 variant `F_MISS` distribution still has two clear bands at
~0.45 and ~0.55 — i.e. another mixture. Pull those band variants out as
a new high-peak set and use them to discriminate the remaining chips.

<details><summary>Show plot — AncestryDNA not_v1 per-variant F_MISS</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/AncestryDNA_not_v1_vmiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
vmiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_not_v1.vmiss')
vmiss <- vmiss[vmiss$F_MISS < 0.9, ]
hist(vmiss$F_MISS, 100,
     main = 'AncestryDNA not_v1: per-variant F_MISS (clipped <0.9)',
     xlab = 'F_MISS')
abline(v = c(0.4, 0.6), col = 'red', lty = 2)
legend('topright', 'notv1_high_peak band: 0.4-0.6',
       col = 'red', lty = 2, bty = 'n')

# Variants in 0.4-0.6 missingness within not_v1 are the discriminating set
# between the remaining chips (v2 vs v3 vs v4).
notv1_high_peak <- vmiss$ID[vmiss$F_MISS > 0.4 & vmiss$F_MISS < 0.6]
write.table(
  notv1_high_peak,
  '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_notv1_high_peak.extract',
  row.names = F, col.names = F, quote = F
)
```

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
# Sample missingness within the not_v1 group restricted to notv1_high_peak variants.

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA \
  --remove ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_v1.keep \
  --extract ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_notv1_high_peak.extract \
  --missing \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_notv1_high_peak

```

</details>

The recomputed `NONMISSING_CT` histogram (excluding the lower tail of
stragglers) shows three tight, well-separated peaks corresponding to
**v2**, **v3** and **v4**. The lower tail (NONMISSING_CT < 125k, ~20
samples) is left unassigned — those samples will be removed by `--mind 0.05`
in the final filter step.

<details><summary>Show plot — AncestryDNA NONMISSING_CT within notv1 high-peak variants</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/AncestryDNA_notv1_high_peak_smiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
smiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_notv1_high_peak.smiss')
smiss$NONMISSING_CT <- smiss$OBS_CT - smiss$MISSING_CT
hist(smiss$NONMISSING_CT[smiss$NONMISSING_CT > 100000], 80,
     main = 'AncestryDNA not_v1: NONMISSING_CT within notv1_high_peak variants\n(three peaks -> v2 / v3 / v4)',
     xlab = 'NONMISSING_CT')
abline(v = c(132500, 142000), col = 'red', lty = 2)
legend('topright',
       c('v2/v3 boundary = 132,500', 'v3/v4 boundary = 142,000'),
       col = 'red', lty = 2, bty = 'n')

# Three tight clusters within not_v1:
#   ~365 samples at ~130k -> v2 (lower NONMISSING_CT in high-peak set)
#   ~310 samples at ~136k -> v3 (middle)
#   ~111 samples at ~147k -> v4 (upper)
# Plus ~20 lower-tail stragglers (<125k) that get dropped by --mind 0.05.
dat_v2 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT >= 125000 & smiss$NONMISSING_CT <  132500]
dat_v3 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT >= 132500 & smiss$NONMISSING_CT <= 142000]
dat_v4 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT >  142000]

write.table(cbind(dat_v2, dat_v2),
            '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_v2.keep',
            row.names = F, col.names = F, quote = F)
write.table(cbind(dat_v3, dat_v3),
            '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_v3.keep',
            row.names = F, col.names = F, quote = F)
write.table(cbind(dat_v4, dat_v4),
            '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_v4.keep',
            row.names = F, col.names = F, quote = F)
```

</details>

### Step 4 — make per-array filesets and apply --geno / --mind

Same recipe as 23andMe: per-array `--make-bed`, then `--geno 0.05`, then
`--mind 0.05`.

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
for version in v1 v2 v3 v4; do
/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA \
  --keep ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_${version}.keep \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_${version}

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_${version} \
  --geno 0.05 \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_${version}_geno

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_${version}_geno \
  --mind 0.05 \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/AncestryDNA_${version}_filtered
done

```

</details>

Per-sub-array variant `F_MISS` after splitting (computed on each
`AncestryDNA_v?.keep` subset *before* `--geno`/`--mind`). Each panel has a
single tight peak near zero plus a small tail — i.e. each sub-array is
behaving like a single clean chip.

<details><summary>Show plot — AncestryDNA per-sub-array variant F_MISS</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/AncestryDNA_per_array_verify_vmiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

***

## FamilyTree

FamilyTreeDNA is the easiest of the three: the per-sample `NONMISSING_CT`
histogram is directly multimodal — no high-peak variant trick needed.
Three clusters are visible and we split on them directly. (The
cross-array variant overlap check at the end of this document confirms
that *only* three groups are warranted, even though an earlier exploratory
split into four sub-arrays looked plausible from the histogram alone — see
the FamilyTreeDNA notes in the Summary section.)

<details><summary>Show plot — FamilyTreeDNA per-sample NONMISSING_CT with thresholds</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/FamilyTreeDNA_smiss_thresholds.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show plot — FamilyTreeDNA per-variant F_MISS</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/FamilyTreeDNA_vmiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
smiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA.smiss')
smiss$NONMISSING_CT <- smiss$OBS_CT - smiss$MISSING_CT
hist(smiss$NONMISSING_CT, 120, xlim = c(550000, 730000),
     main = 'FamilyTreeDNA: per-sample non-missing count\nthree clusters -> v1 / v2 / v3',
     xlab = 'NONMISSING_CT')
abline(v = c(580000, 610000, 650000), col = 'red', lty = 2)
legend('topleft',
       c('lower-tail cutoff = 580,000',
         'v1/v2 boundary = 610,000',
         'sparse/dense gap = 650,000'),
       col = 'red', lty = 2, bty = 'n')

vmiss <- fread('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA.vmiss')
vmiss <- vmiss[!is.na(vmiss$F_MISS) & vmiss$F_MISS < 0.99, ]
hist(vmiss$F_MISS, 100,
     main = 'FamilyTreeDNA: per-variant F_MISS (clipped <0.99)',
     xlab = 'F_MISS')

# Three clusters kept, plus a lower tail dropped by --mind 0.05 below:
#   ~72  samples at 580-610k -> v1 (sparse, lower)
#   ~58  samples at 610-650k -> v2 (sparse, upper)
#   ~358 samples at >=650k   -> v3 (dense)
dat_v1 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT >= 580000 & smiss$NONMISSING_CT <  610000]
dat_v2 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT >= 610000 & smiss$NONMISSING_CT <  650000]
dat_v3 <- smiss$IID[smiss$NONMISSING_CT >= 650000]

write.table(cbind(dat_v1, dat_v1),
            '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_v1.keep',
            row.names = F, col.names = F, quote = F)
write.table(cbind(dat_v2, dat_v2),
            '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_v2.keep',
            row.names = F, col.names = F, quote = F)
write.table(cbind(dat_v3, dat_v3),
            '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_v3.keep',
            row.names = F, col.names = F, quote = F)
```

</details>

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
# Make per-array beds and apply --geno 0.05 then --mind 0.05 (same recipe as 23andMe)

for version in v1 v2 v3; do
/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA \
  --keep ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_${version}.keep \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_${version}

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_${version} \
  --geno 0.05 \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_${version}_geno

/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_${version}_geno \
  --mind 0.05 \
  --make-bed \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/FamilyTreeDNA_${version}_filtered
done

```

</details>

Per-sub-array variant `F_MISS` after splitting. v3 is clean (single tight
peak near zero); v1 and v2 still show some bimodality, indicating the
*sparse* group probably contains low-grade chip-version heterogeneity that
the sample histogram doesn't resolve. The residual is absorbed by
`--geno 0.05`, which only keeps variants present in ≥95% of each
sub-array's samples — and `--mind 0.05` drops just one sample across all
three sub-arrays, so the splits are usable as-is.

<details><summary>Show plot — FamilyTreeDNA per-sub-array variant F_MISS</summary>

<div class="centered-container">
<div class="rounded-image-container" style="width: 100%;">
<img src="Images/opensnp_expanded/FamilyTreeDNA_per_array_verify_vmiss.png">
</div>
</div>

</details>

***

## Summary

### Per-array sample and variant counts

After all splitting and filtering, the per-array sample (`n_ind`) and
variant (`n_var`) counts are summarised by the chunk below. The relevant
output is included as a static table immediately afterwards so the
document is readable without re-running the chunks.

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
library(data.table)
# Check sample size and number of variants in filtered and unfiltered versions of each dataset
datasets <- c('23andme','23andme_v3','23andme_v4','23andme_v5',
              'AncestryDNA','AncestryDNA_v1','AncestryDNA_v2','AncestryDNA_v3','AncestryDNA_v4',
              'FamilyTreeDNA','FamilyTreeDNA_v1','FamilyTreeDNA_v2','FamilyTreeDNA_v3')
types <- c('filtered','unfiltered')

stats <- NULL
for(i in datasets){
  for(j in types){
    tmp_bim <- fread(
      paste0(
        '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/',
        i,
        ifelse(j == 'filtered', '_filtered', ''),
        '.bim'
      ),
      header = F
    )
    tmp_fam <- fread(
      paste0(
        '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/',
        i,
        ifelse(j == 'filtered', '_filtered', ''),
        '.fam'
      ),
      header = F
    )
    stats <- rbind(
      stats,
      data.frame(
        dataset = i,
        type = j,
        n_var = nrow(tmp_bim),
        n_ind = nrow(tmp_fam)
      )
    )
  }
}

# A lot more variants are retained per array than in the company-wide
# `_filtered` files shipped in the Zenodo archive, because we no longer
# have to take the intersection across chips.
```

</details>

Snapshot of the `_filtered` per-array counts produced above:

| array | n_ind | n_var |
|---|---:|---:|
| AncestryDNA_v1_filtered | 331 | 675,445 |
| AncestryDNA_v2_filtered | 364 | 650,609 |
| AncestryDNA_v3_filtered | 310 | 680,327 |
| AncestryDNA_v4_filtered | 111 | 663,343 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v1_filtered | 72 | 561,522 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v2_filtered | 57 | 523,207 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v3_filtered | 358 | 662,827 |

(23andMe sample counts are produced by the chunk above — these depend on
the exact `--mind 0.05` cut and aren't pinned in this static snapshot. The
23andMe `_filtered` variant counts are 933,618 (v3), 459,381 (v4) and
585,145 (v5).)

### Variant overlap across sub-arrays

The single best sanity check that a per-company split actually captured
different chips — rather than two density tiers of the same chip — is the
**variant overlap matrix** across sub-arrays. After `--geno 0.05` each
sub-array has been reduced to its own clean variant set; if two sub-arrays
share ~all of their variants, they are the same chip and should be merged.

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{r}
# Sanity-check the sub-array splits: how many variants survive the per-array
# --geno 0.05 filter in *every* sub-array of a given company? A clean split
# leaves a large shared backbone but each sub-array also has chip-specific
# variants that the others don't.

library(data.table)

groups <- list(
  '23andme'       = c('23andme_v3','23andme_v4','23andme_v5'),
  'AncestryDNA'   = c('AncestryDNA_v1','AncestryDNA_v2','AncestryDNA_v3','AncestryDNA_v4'),
  'FamilyTreeDNA' = c('FamilyTreeDNA_v1','FamilyTreeDNA_v2','FamilyTreeDNA_v3')
)

overlap <- NULL
for (company in names(groups)) {
  vars <- lapply(groups[[company]], function(x)
    fread(paste0('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/', x, '_filtered.bim'),
          header = F)$V2
  )
  names(vars) <- groups[[company]]
  shared_all <- Reduce(intersect, vars)
  union_all  <- Reduce(union, vars)
  overlap <- rbind(overlap, data.frame(
    company        = company,
    n_arrays       = length(vars),
    per_array_n    = paste(sapply(vars, length), collapse = '/'),
    shared_all     = length(shared_all),
    union_all      = length(union_all),
    shared_frac_min = round(length(shared_all) / min(sapply(vars, length)), 3)
  ))
}
print(overlap)

# Pairwise overlap within each company (helpful to see *which* sub-array is
# the odd one out, if any).
for (company in names(groups)) {
  vars <- lapply(groups[[company]], function(x)
    fread(paste0('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/', x, '_filtered.bim'),
          header = F)$V2
  )
  names(vars) <- groups[[company]]
  m <- outer(seq_along(vars), seq_along(vars),
             Vectorize(function(i, j) length(intersect(vars[[i]], vars[[j]]))))
  dimnames(m) <- list(names(vars), names(vars))
  cat('\n', company, '— pairwise variant overlap:\n', sep = '')
  print(m)
}
```

</details>

#### Per-company summary

| company | n_arrays | per-array variants | shared across all | shared / smallest |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|
| 23andme | 3 | 933,618 / 459,381 / 585,145 | 105,451 | 0.230 |
| AncestryDNA | 4 | 675,445 / 650,609 / 680,327 / 663,343 | 390,448 | 0.600 |
| FamilyTreeDNA | 3 | 561,522 / 523,207 / 662,827 | 153,826 | 0.292 |

#### Pairwise overlap matrices

**23andMe** — the OmniExpress backbone is shared by v3 and v4 (453k); v5
(GSA) is genuinely different from both:

|  | 23andme_v3 | 23andme_v4 | 23andme_v5 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| 23andme_v3 | 933,618 | 452,737 | 189,684 |
| 23andme_v4 | 452,737 | 459,381 | 106,485 |
| 23andme_v5 | 189,684 | 106,485 | 585,145 |

**AncestryDNA** — all four chips share a 390k backbone, and pairwise
overlaps are 414–641k. v3 and v4 share the most (641k of 663–680k), but
each still has tens of thousands of chip-specific variants, so they are
genuine separate chips, not duplicates:

|  | v1 | v2 | v3 | v4 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| AncestryDNA_v1 | 675,445 | 414,794 | 441,568 | 433,898 |
| AncestryDNA_v2 | 414,794 | 650,609 | 520,344 | 507,753 |
| AncestryDNA_v3 | 441,568 | 520,344 | 680,327 | 641,340 |
| AncestryDNA_v4 | 433,898 | 507,753 | 641,340 | 663,343 |

**FamilyTreeDNA** — two genuinely different chips: the *sparse* family
(v1 + v2) shares 486k variants out of 523–561k (~93%), and v1 ∩ v2 is
much higher than either's overlap with v3 (only ~155–199k). The *dense*
group used to be split further into a v3/v4 pair by sample missingness,
but the pairwise overlap was 656k of 657–683k (99.8%) — meaning that
"v4" was essentially a slightly higher-density version of the same chip.
Those two were therefore merged into a single v3.

|  | v1 | v2 | v3 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| FamilyTreeDNA_v1 | 561,522 | 486,260 | 199,085 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v2 | 486,260 | 523,207 | 154,163 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v3 | 199,085 | 154,163 | 662,827 |

(v1 and v2 are kept distinct because they carry meaningfully different
variant counts — 561k vs 523k — and the within-pair overlap is "only"
~93%, not the 99.8% seen for the merged v3/v4 dense group.)


***

# Prepare for Michigan Imputation Server

The next step before polygenic scoring is **imputation**. Each per-array
fileset is uploaded to the Michigan Imputation Server's
HRC r1.1 GRCh37 panel separately, since each chip has its own variant
backbone. The server requires per-chromosome bgzipped VCFs whose REF
alleles already match the panel; this section produces them.

Reference panel: **HRC r1.1, GRCh37**. The OpenSNP per-array filesets are
already on GRCh37 (chr22 max position ≈ 51.2 Mb), so no liftover is
required. The Michigan Server's official prep instructions
(<https://imputationserver.readthedocs.io/en/latest/prepare-your-data/>)
recommend Will Rayner's `HRC-1000G-check-bim.pl` for strand / REF-allele
alignment against the panel sites file, and that's exactly what we use
here. The recipe mirrors the user's older
`Michigan-Imputation-Helper/MichPrep.r`
(<https://github.com/opain/Michigan-Imputation-Helper>) but inlined as
shell chunks so the document is self-contained.

## Dependencies

One-off downloads / installs in the working environment. All paths below
assume the binaries land in `/tmp/` and the HRC sites file lands in
`~/oliverpainfel/Data/HRC/`. If you've already installed any of these,
skip the corresponding chunk.

<details><summary>Show code — install PLINK 1.9, bcftools, htslib (bgzip/tabix)</summary>

```{bash}
# PLINK 1.9 — required by HRC-check-bim's generated Run-plink.sh (uses
# 1.9-specific flags like --a2-allele) and by the per-chromosome --recode
# vcf step. Use a current build from cog-genomics's S3 bucket.
cd /tmp
curl -sL -o plink1.zip \
  "https://s3.amazonaws.com/plink1-assets/plink_linux_x86_64_20231211.zip"
unzip -o plink1.zip plink prettify
chmod +x /tmp/plink
/tmp/plink --version

# bcftools (and htslib's bgzip + tabix) — needed to sort and bgzip the
# per-chromosome VCFs. Build from source if no system package is
# available.
cd /tmp
curl -sL -o bcftools.tar.bz2 \
  "https://github.com/samtools/bcftools/releases/download/1.20/bcftools-1.20.tar.bz2"
tar -xjf bcftools.tar.bz2
cd bcftools-1.20
./configure --prefix=/tmp/bcftools_install
make -j4
# bgzip / tabix live in the htslib subdir; build them too
cd htslib-1.20
make bgzip tabix
# Convenience symlinks
ln -sf /tmp/bcftools-1.20/bcftools          /tmp/bcftools
ln -sf /tmp/bcftools-1.20/htslib-1.20/bgzip /tmp/bgzip
ln -sf /tmp/bcftools-1.20/htslib-1.20/tabix /tmp/tabix
/tmp/bcftools --version | head -1
/tmp/bgzip   --version | head -1
/tmp/tabix   --version | head -1
```

</details>

<details><summary>Show code — download HRC-1000G-check-bim.pl + HRC r1.1 sites file</summary>

```{bash}
# Will Rayner's check script (current is v4.3.0 as of 2026)
mkdir -p /tmp/hrc_check
cd /tmp/hrc_check
curl -sL -o hrc_check.zip \
  "https://www.chg.ox.ac.uk/~wrayner/tools/HRC-1000G-check-bim-v4.3.0.zip"
unzip -o hrc_check.zip
ls /tmp/hrc_check/HRC-1000G-check-bim.pl

# HRC r1.1 GRCh37 sites file — ~700 MB (BGZF gzipped). The Rayner script
# reads .gz natively (uses IO::Uncompress::Gunzip) so no need to
# decompress.
mkdir -p ~/oliverpainfel/Data/HRC
cd ~/oliverpainfel/Data/HRC
curl -sL --max-time 1800 -o HRC.r1-1.GRCh37.wgs.mac5.sites.tab.gz \
  "ftp://ngs.sanger.ac.uk/production/hrc/HRC.r1-1/HRC.r1-1.GRCh37.wgs.mac5.sites.tab.gz"
ls -la ~/oliverpainfel/Data/HRC/HRC.r1-1.GRCh37.wgs.mac5.sites.tab.gz
```

</details>

## Per-array prep loop

For each of the 10 per-array filesets, the recipe is:

1. **Pre-clean**: drop indels (`--snps-only just-acgt`), restrict to
   autosomes (`--chr 1-22`), drop multi-position duplicates
   (`--rm-dup exclude-all`), and force bi-allelic
   (`--max-alleles 2`). The OpenSNP raw filesets contain ~14k indel /
   non-ACGT alleles and an X+Y chromosome that the HRC track doesn't
   cover.
2. **Allele frequencies**: `plink --freq`, needed by the Rayner script
   to detect AF-mismatch variants.
3. **HRC alignment check**: `perl HRC-1000G-check-bim.pl -h` against the
   HRC sites file. This generates `Run-plink.sh` plus per-action variant
   lists (`Exclude-…`, `Strand-Flip-…`, `Force-Allele1-…`,
   `Position-…`, `Chromosome-…`, `ID-…`).
4. **Apply alignment**: `bash Run-plink.sh` — applies the exclusions,
   strand flips, REF-allele forcing, and splits per chromosome. Writes
   `…_clean-updated-chr<1..22>.{bed,bim,fam,vcf}`.
5. **Sort + bgzip**: `bcftools sort -Oz` per chromosome → final
   `<prefix>-chr<N>.vcf.gz` ready for upload.

The Rayner script's `Run-plink.sh` writes `plink ` (bare); we `sed` it
to `/tmp/plink ` so the loop uses the binary installed above. Skip this
substitution if `plink` is already on `$PATH`.

<details><summary>Show code — full prep loop over all 10 per-array filesets</summary>

```{bash}
DATADIR=~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898
HRCREF=~/oliverpainfel/Data/HRC/HRC.r1-1.GRCh37.wgs.mac5.sites.tab.gz
WORKDIR=$DATADIR/imputation_prep
mkdir -p $WORKDIR/temp

run_prep () {
  local prefix=$1
  echo "===== $prefix ====="

  # Step 0 — pre-clean
  /tmp/plink2 \
    --bfile $DATADIR/${prefix}_filtered \
    --snps-only just-acgt --chr 1-22 \
    --rm-dup exclude-all --max-alleles 2 \
    --make-bed --out $WORKDIR/${prefix}_clean

  # Step 1 — allele frequencies
  /tmp/plink \
    --bfile $WORKDIR/${prefix}_clean \
    --freq --out $WORKDIR/${prefix}_clean

  # Step 2 — HRC alignment check (writes Run-plink.sh + LOG-*.txt + ...)
  cd $WORKDIR
  perl /tmp/hrc_check/HRC-1000G-check-bim.pl \
    -b $WORKDIR/${prefix}_clean.bim \
    -f $WORKDIR/${prefix}_clean.frq \
    -r $HRCREF \
    -h

  # Substitute the plink binary path in the generated script
  sed -i 's|^plink |/tmp/plink |g; s| plink | /tmp/plink |g' Run-plink.sh

  # Step 3 — apply alignment (also splits per chromosome and emits VCFs)
  bash Run-plink.sh

  # Step 4 — sort + bgzip per chromosome
  for chr in $(seq 1 22); do
    /tmp/bcftools sort \
      $WORKDIR/${prefix}_clean-updated-chr${chr}.vcf \
      -Oz -o $WORKDIR/${prefix}-chr${chr}.vcf.gz
  done
}

for prefix in 23andme_v3 23andme_v4 23andme_v5 \
              AncestryDNA_v1 AncestryDNA_v2 AncestryDNA_v3 AncestryDNA_v4 \
              FamilyTreeDNA_v1 FamilyTreeDNA_v2 FamilyTreeDNA_v3; do
  run_prep "$prefix"
done

# Optional cleanup: keep the LOG-*.txt files for provenance, drop the
# rest. The .vcf.gz files in $WORKDIR are the upload payload.
rm -f $WORKDIR/*_clean-updated-chr*.{bed,bim,fam,vcf,log,nosex} \
      $WORKDIR/*_clean-updated.{bed,bim,fam,log,nosex} \
      $WORKDIR/TEMP*.{bed,bim,fam,log,nosex}
```

</details>

## What the Rayner check did

The HRC alignment check (`HRC-1000G-check-bim.pl -h`) reports, per
fileset, how many variants it had to flip, exclude, swap REF/ALT for, or
re-position. The numbers come from the tail of the script's stdout (also
written to `LOG-<prefix>_clean-HRC.txt` in the prep dir). A clean run
typically excludes <5% of variants; a higher figure indicates a strand
or build issue worth investigating.

(Static snapshot from the prep loop above — re-run the loop to refresh.)

| prefix | total in bim | REF/ALT swap | strand flip | no match | AF diff > 0.2 | palindromic high-freq | non-matching alleles |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 23andme_v3 | 890,777 | 622,924 | 0 | 17,617 | 387 | 889 | 4,244 |
| 23andme_v4 | 431,811 | 302,782 | 0 | 1,217 | 98 | 172 | 1,005 |
| 23andme_v5 | 557,346 | 446,321 | 0 | 27,117 | 767 | 241 | 14,032 |
| AncestryDNA_v1 | 652,874 | 462,569 | 0 | 5,306 | 207 | 21 | 5,916 |
| AncestryDNA_v2 | 617,204 | 406,712 | 0 | 43,487 | 1,315 | 819 | 7,855 |
| AncestryDNA_v3 | 648,579 | 424,998 | 0 | 45,819 | 429 | 2,419 | 7,323 |
| AncestryDNA_v4 | 632,406 | 416,057 | 0 | 41,597 | 454 | 2,392 | 6,900 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v1 | 555,386 | 408,055 | 0 | 0 | 2,520 | 290 | 64,648 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v2 | 506,161 | 366,384 | 0 | 24,910 | 467 | 250 | 51,538 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v3 | 656,690 | 460,997 | 0 | 6,747 | 216 | 275 | 9,227 |

A few notes on the numbers:

- **Strand flips = 0 across the board.** The OpenSNP raw filesets are
  already on the forward strand, so no flipping was needed.
- **REF/ALT swaps are large** (~65–80% of variants per fileset). This is
  expected: PLINK's default A1/A2 ordering is minor/major allele, while
  HRC uses REF/ALT. The Rayner script's `Force-Allele1-…txt` corrects
  this via `plink --a2-allele`.
- **"No match to HRC"** varies from 0 (FamilyTreeDNA_v1) to ~46k
  (AncestryDNA_v3). These are array-specific custom variants —
  predominantly chip-specific GWAS markers — that aren't in HRC and so
  can't be imputed against HRC. They're dropped from the upload.
- **FamilyTreeDNA_v1 and v2 have unusually high "non-matching alleles"
  counts** (~65k and ~52k, ~10–12% of their variants). These are
  variants where the bim alleles don't match HRC's REF/ALT even after
  strand-flip consideration — typical of older or more esoteric chips.
  Imputation will still work but with a smaller usable variant set.

***

## Verify outputs

A short check that every per-array fileset produced 22 non-empty
per-chromosome VCFs with sane variant counts (chr1 should be the largest
file, chr22 the smallest, etc).

<details><summary>Show code — list per-chromosome variant counts</summary>

```{r}
library(data.table)
DATADIR <- '~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/imputation_prep'
prefixes <- c('23andme_v3','23andme_v4','23andme_v5',
              'AncestryDNA_v1','AncestryDNA_v2','AncestryDNA_v3','AncestryDNA_v4',
              'FamilyTreeDNA_v1','FamilyTreeDNA_v2','FamilyTreeDNA_v3')

counts <- NULL
for (p in prefixes) {
  for (chr in 1:22) {
    f <- file.path(DATADIR, sprintf('%s-chr%d.vcf.gz', p, chr))
    if (!file.exists(f)) {
      counts <- rbind(counts, data.frame(prefix = p, chr = chr,
                                          n_var = NA, file_kb = NA))
      next
    }
    n <- as.integer(system(sprintf('/tmp/bcftools view -H %s | wc -l', f),
                           intern = TRUE))
    counts <- rbind(counts, data.frame(
      prefix  = p,
      chr     = chr,
      n_var   = n,
      file_kb = round(file.info(f)$size / 1024)
    ))
  }
}

# Wide table: rows = prefix, columns = chr
mat <- dcast(as.data.table(counts), prefix ~ chr, value.var = 'n_var')
print(mat)

# Totals
totals <- counts[, .(n_var = sum(n_var, na.rm = TRUE),
                     n_chr = sum(!is.na(n_var))),
                 by = prefix, env = list(prefix = 'prefix')]
print(totals)
```

</details>

(Static snapshot of the per-prefix totals from the run above; chr1
shown as the largest, chr11 mid-size, chr22 the smallest:)

| prefix | total variants | chr1 | chr11 | chr22 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 23andme_v3 | 867,640 | 71,239 | 44,249 | 12,808 |
| 23andme_v4 | 429,319 | 34,015 | 21,186 | 6,459 |
| 23andme_v5 | 515,189 | 40,946 | 25,681 | 7,259 |
| AncestryDNA_v1 | 641,424 | 53,489 | 33,232 | 9,279 |
| AncestryDNA_v2 | 563,728 | 44,778 | 27,414 | 9,192 |
| AncestryDNA_v3 | 592,589 | 46,219 | 28,587 | 10,059 |
| AncestryDNA_v4 | 581,063 | 45,231 | 28,127 | 9,914 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v1 | 487,928 | 38,099 | 25,811 | 7,060 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v2 | 428,996 | 33,348 | 23,770 | 5,596 |
| FamilyTreeDNA_v3 | 640,225 | 53,495 | 33,241 | 9,348 |

All ten prefixes produced the expected 22 per-chromosome `.vcf.gz`
files. Per-chromosome counts scale roughly with chromosome length, as
expected.

Once these files exist, upload each `<prefix>` set (22 files) to the
Michigan Imputation Server with the **HRC r1.1 2016** reference panel
selected. The server runs its own input-QC report on upload — the
per-fileset Rayner `LOG-<prefix>_clean-HRC.txt` file in
`imputation_prep/` is your local copy of essentially the same checks.


***

# Submit to the TOPMed Imputation Server via API

The Michigan Imputation Server has been intermittent; the
[TOPMed Imputation Server](https://imputation.biodatacatalyst.nhlbi.nih.gov/)
uses the same input format and a slightly more modern reference panel
(TOPMed r3, GRCh38). Two things to know up-front:

- The **same per-array `.vcf.gz` files** produced above are usable
  directly. The server accepts GRCh37 input with integer chromosome
  names (no `chr` prefix) — set `build=hg19` and the server lifts to
  GRCh38 internally. We do **not** need to redo any prep.
- There is no documented REST endpoint for *downloading* results
  (per the TOPMed docs); only per-chromosome wget links provided in the
  web UI's **Results** tab. The submit / monitor steps are full API,
  but the download step is semi-manual — you paste the wget commands
  into a script and run them on the HPC.

API basics (per <https://topmedimpute.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/>):

- Base URL: `https://imputation.biodatacatalyst.nhlbi.nih.gov/api/v2`
- Auth header: `X-Auth-Token: <TOKEN>` — generate the token from the
  user profile page (`/#!pages/profile`); it expires after 30 days.
- Limits: **3 concurrent jobs per user**, max 25,000 samples per
  chromosome per job, results auto-deleted after **7 days**.
- We will submit **one job per per-array fileset** (10 jobs total);
  each job carries 22 chromosome VCFs.

## Step 1 — get an API token and store it locally

Generate the token via the web UI (Profile → API → Generate token),
then drop it into a permission-protected file in your home directory.
Don't commit it.

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
# Save your TOPMed API token outside the repo, readable only by you
mkdir -p ~/.config/topmed
umask 077
printf "%s" "PASTE-YOUR-TOKEN-HERE" > ~/.config/topmed/api_token
chmod 600 ~/.config/topmed/api_token
ls -la ~/.config/topmed/api_token
```

</details>

## Step 2 — submit one job per per-array fileset

The submit endpoint is `POST /jobs/submit/imputationserver`,
multipart, with one `-F "files=@..."` per chromosome VCF. The reference
panel ID for TOPMed r3 is `apps@topmed-r3`; we set `build=hg19` (input
is GRCh37) and `phasing=eagle`, `population=all` (the full TOPMed
panel — appropriate for OpenSNP's mixed ancestry).

Quota: max 3 jobs running at once, so the loop submits in batches of 3
and waits for the current batch to leave the queue before submitting
the next. The job ID returned by each successful submit is appended to
`~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/imputation_prep/topmed_jobs.tsv`
for the monitor / download steps.

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
TOKEN=$(cat ~/.config/topmed/api_token)
API=https://imputation.biodatacatalyst.nhlbi.nih.gov/api/v2
WORKDIR=~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/imputation_prep
JOBS_TSV=$WORKDIR/topmed_jobs.tsv

PREFIXES=(23andme_v3 23andme_v4 23andme_v5 \
          AncestryDNA_v1 AncestryDNA_v2 AncestryDNA_v3 AncestryDNA_v4 \
          FamilyTreeDNA_v1 FamilyTreeDNA_v2 FamilyTreeDNA_v3)

# Header (only if the file doesn't exist)
[ -f "$JOBS_TSV" ] || printf "prefix\tjob_id\tsubmitted_utc\n" > $JOBS_TSV

# How many of *my* jobs are in waiting/running/exporting state?
n_active () {
  curl -s -H "X-Auth-Token: $TOKEN" "$API/jobs" \
    | jq '[.data[] | select(.state==1 or .state==2 or .state==3)] | length'
}

submit_one () {
  local prefix=$1
  echo "Submitting $prefix ..."
  # Build the -F args: one per chromosome VCF
  local args=()
  for chr in $(seq 1 22); do
    args+=( -F "files=@${WORKDIR}/${prefix}-chr${chr}.vcf.gz" )
  done
  local resp
  resp=$(curl -s -X POST -H "X-Auth-Token: $TOKEN" \
    -F "job-name=opensnp_${prefix}" \
    -F "refpanel=apps@topmed-r3" \
    -F "build=hg19" \
    -F "phasing=eagle" \
    -F "population=all" \
    -F "meta=yes" \
    "${args[@]}" \
    "$API/jobs/submit/imputationserver")
  local id=$(echo "$resp" | jq -r '.id')
  if [ "$id" = "null" ] || [ -z "$id" ]; then
    echo "  FAILED: $resp"
    return 1
  fi
  printf "%s\t%s\t%s\n" "$prefix" "$id" "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" >> $JOBS_TSV
  echo "  job id: $id"
}

# Submit, respecting the 3-concurrent-jobs quota
for prefix in "${PREFIXES[@]}"; do
  # Skip if already submitted (resume-friendly)
  if grep -q "^${prefix}\t" "$JOBS_TSV"; then
    echo "Already submitted: $prefix — skipping"
    continue
  fi
  # Wait until <3 active jobs
  while [ "$(n_active)" -ge 3 ]; do
    echo "$(date -u +%H:%M:%S) — 3 jobs active, sleeping 90s"
    sleep 90
  done
  submit_one "$prefix"
  sleep 5  # be polite between submits
done

cat $JOBS_TSV
```

</details>

## Step 3 — monitor job status until all 10 complete

`GET /jobs/{id}/status` returns the integer `state`:
`1`=waiting, `2`=running, `3`=exporting, `4`=success, `5`=failed,
`6`=canceled. Poll **no more than once per minute** (per the docs).
The loop below prints a one-line per-job status update each minute and
exits when every job is in state ≥4.

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
TOKEN=$(cat ~/.config/topmed/api_token)
API=https://imputation.biodatacatalyst.nhlbi.nih.gov/api/v2
JOBS_TSV=~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/imputation_prep/topmed_jobs.tsv

state_label () {
  case "$1" in
    1) echo "waiting";;
    2) echo "running";;
    3) echo "exporting";;
    4) echo "SUCCESS";;
    5) echo "FAILED";;
    6) echo "canceled";;
    *) echo "unknown($1)";;
  esac
}

while true; do
  all_done=1
  echo "=== $(date -u +%H:%M:%SZ) ==="
  while IFS=$'\t' read -r prefix id submitted; do
    [ "$prefix" = "prefix" ] && continue  # header
    s=$(curl -s -H "X-Auth-Token: $TOKEN" "$API/jobs/${id}/status" \
        | jq -r '.state')
    printf "  %-25s %s  %s\n" "$prefix" "$id" "$(state_label $s)"
    [ "$s" -lt 4 ] && all_done=0
  done < $JOBS_TSV
  [ $all_done -eq 1 ] && { echo "All jobs finished."; break; }
  sleep 60
done
```

</details>

## Step 4 — collect the wget commands from the web UI

The TOPMed API does not expose a download endpoint; the docs direct
users to the **Results** tab in the web UI for each job, click
**Share** → **wget command**, and paste the per-chromosome wget
commands into a script. Repeat per job (10 jobs).

Save the lot to a single file. Each line will look roughly like:

```
wget -O chr_1.zip "https://imputation.biodatacatalyst.nhlbi.nih.gov/share/results/<HASH>/chr_1.zip"
```

We organise downloads into one subdirectory per prefix so files don't
clobber each other (every job writes `chr_1.zip` … `chr_22.zip`).

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
RESULTS_ROOT=~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/imputation_results
mkdir -p $RESULTS_ROOT

# For each prefix, paste the wget commands from the UI into the file
# below (one wget per chromosome, plus the info file and any QC reports).
# The harness then cd's into the right subdir before running them.

for prefix in 23andme_v3 23andme_v4 23andme_v5 \
              AncestryDNA_v1 AncestryDNA_v2 AncestryDNA_v3 AncestryDNA_v4 \
              FamilyTreeDNA_v1 FamilyTreeDNA_v2 FamilyTreeDNA_v3; do
  mkdir -p $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix
  # Place the per-prefix wget script at $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix/wget_commands.sh
  # (one line per file, pasted from the web UI)
  if [ -f $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix/wget_commands.sh ]; then
    echo "=== Downloading $prefix ==="
    ( cd $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix && bash wget_commands.sh )
  else
    echo "No wget_commands.sh for $prefix — paste the UI's wget commands into $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix/wget_commands.sh and re-run"
  fi
done
```

</details>

## Step 5 — decrypt the per-chromosome result archives

Every results `.zip` is encrypted with a one-time password emailed to
you when the job completes (the password is **not** returned by the
API). Default mode is standard PKZIP (use `unzip -P`); if you opted
into AES-256 at submit time (`aesEncryption=yes`, off by default), use
`7z x -p…` instead.

The loop below decrypts every `.zip` it finds in the per-prefix result
dirs into a sibling `vcf/` subdirectory. The password is read from
`~/.config/topmed/zip_password_<prefix>` so it stays out of the shell
history; create one such file per job and `chmod 600` it.

<details><summary>Show code</summary>

```{bash}
RESULTS_ROOT=~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_18493898/imputation_results

for prefix in 23andme_v3 23andme_v4 23andme_v5 \
              AncestryDNA_v1 AncestryDNA_v2 AncestryDNA_v3 AncestryDNA_v4 \
              FamilyTreeDNA_v1 FamilyTreeDNA_v2 FamilyTreeDNA_v3; do
  PWFILE=~/.config/topmed/zip_password_${prefix}
  if [ ! -f "$PWFILE" ]; then
    echo "No password file for $prefix at $PWFILE — skipping"
    continue
  fi
  PW=$(cat "$PWFILE")
  mkdir -p $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix/vcf
  for z in $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix/chr_*.zip; do
    [ -f "$z" ] || continue
    echo "Unzipping $(basename $z) for $prefix"
    unzip -o -P "$PW" "$z" -d $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix/vcf
  done
  ls $RESULTS_ROOT/$prefix/vcf/ | head
done
```

</details>

After this you have, per prefix, a `vcf/` directory containing
`chr<N>.dose.vcf.gz`, `chr<N>.info.gz` and `chr<N>.dose.vcf.gz.tbi` —
the imputed dosages, the per-variant info / R² file, and a tabix index
— all on **GRCh38** (the panel build) regardless of what build you
sent in.

## Notes / caveats

- **Token expiry.** Tokens expire 30 days after generation; if your
  monitor loop suddenly returns `"You don't have permission"` errors,
  regenerate via the profile page and update `~/.config/topmed/api_token`.
- **Quotas.** 3 concurrent jobs is a hard cap, so the full 10-job batch
  takes at least ~4 rounds of imputation queue time; expect the whole
  set to take a day or two depending on server load.
- **Result retention.** TOPMed deletes results after **7 days** (with
  a reminder email at 48 h). Don't queue jobs over a long weekend
  unless you can download the results before they expire.
- **No bulk download.** If TOPMed eventually adds a REST download
  endpoint, replace the wget-paste step with a clean per-file
  `GET /jobs/{id}/results/<file>` call. As of writing, it's UI-only.

# Oleksandr Frei

***

## Check missingness

```{bash}
/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/plink2 \
  --bfile ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_10715132/imputed/opensnp_hm3 \
  --missing \
  --out ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_10715132/imputed/opensnp_hm3
```

```{r}
library(dat)
vmiss <- fread('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_10715132/imputed/opensnp_hm3.vmiss')
smiss <- fread('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_10715132/imputed/opensnp_hm3.smiss')
# There are fewer individuals
# There is almost no missing data, which means the imputed dosages had a high certainty.
# It would probably be fine to use this version of the data for downstream PGS.
```

***

## Prepare for GenoPred

***

### Download Howe sumstats

```{bash}
# Download and format within-sib GWAS of height
# https://opengwas.io/datasets/ieu-b-4813#
cd /users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022

for vcf in *.vcf.gz; do
    base=$(basename "$vcf" .vcf.gz)
    output="${base}.txt.gz"

    # Skip if the output file already exists
    if [ -f "$output" ]; then
        echo "Skipping $base: $output already exists."
        continue
    fi

    echo "Processing $base (Setting ALT as A1)..."

    # Header: A1 = Effect Allele, A2 = Non-Effect Allele
    echo -e "CHR\tPOS\tSNP\tA1\tA2\tAF\tBETA\tSE\tP\tN" > "${base}.txt"

    # Stream the VCF, skip headers, and reorder alleles
    zgrep -v "^#" "$vcf" | awk 'BEGIN {FS="\t"; OFS="\t"} {
        # 1. Extract Allele Frequency from INFO
        match($8, /AF=[^;]*/);
        af = substr($8, RSTART+3, RLENGTH-3);
        if (af == "") af = "NA";

        # 2. Split the sample data column (ES:SE:LP:SS:ID)
        split($10, a, ":");
        beta = a[1];
        se   = a[2];
        lp   = a[3];
        ss   = a[4];

        # 3. Convert -log10(p) to raw p-value
        p_val = 10^-lp;

        # 4. Print columns: 
        # $5 is ALT (now A1), $4 is REF (now A2)
        print $1, $2, $3, $5, $4, af, beta, se, p_val, ss
    }' >> "${base}.txt"

    # Compress the output
    gzip "${base}.txt"
    
    echo "Successfully formatted $base."
done
```

***

### Download UKB sumstats

```{bash}
# Define header
cd ~/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab

for gwas in $(echo 50_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 21001_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 4080_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 22501_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 30780_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.varorder.tsv.bgz 20160.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 1558.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 30760_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.varorder.tsv.bgz 20127_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz); do

wget https://broad-ukb-sumstats-us-east-1.s3.amazonaws.com/round2/additive-tsvs/${gwas} -O ${gwas}

done

# Process
cd ~/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab

for file in *.tsv.bgz; do
  base=$(basename "$file" .tsv.bgz)
  output="${base}.clean.tsv"

  if [ -f "$output" ]; then
    echo "Skipping $base: $output already exists."
    continue
  fi

  echo "Processing $base..."

  echo -e "CHR\tPOS\tSNP\tA1\tA2\tEAF\tBETA\tSE\tP\tN" > "$output"

  ~/oliverpainfel/Software/pigz -dc "$file" | \
  awk -v file="$file" '
    BEGIN {
      FS = "\t"
      OFS = "\t"
    }

    NR == 1 {
      for (i = 1; i <= NF; i++) {
        col = $i
        gsub(/^#/, "", col)
        h[col] = i
      }

      required[1] = "variant"
      required[2] = "minor_allele"
      required[3] = "minor_AF"
      required[4] = "low_confidence_variant"
      required[5] = "n_complete_samples"
      required[6] = "beta"
      required[7] = "se"
      required[8] = "pval"

      for (i in required) {
        if (!(required[i] in h)) {
          print "ERROR in " file ": missing required column " required[i] > "/dev/stderr"
          exit 1
        }
      }

      next
    }

    {
      variant = $(h["variant"])
      minor_allele = $(h["minor_allele"])
      minor_AF = $(h["minor_AF"])
      low_conf = $(h["low_confidence_variant"])
      n = $(h["n_complete_samples"])
      beta = $(h["beta"])
      se = $(h["se"])
      p = $(h["pval"])

      if (low_conf == "true" || low_conf == "TRUE" || low_conf == "1") {
        next
      }

      split(variant, v, ":")
      chr = v[1]
      pos = v[2]
      ref = v[3]
      alt = v[4]

      if (chr == "" || pos == "" || ref == "" || alt == "") {
        next
      }

      # Neale Lab additive GWAS beta is with respect to ALT allele
      a1 = alt
      a2 = ref

      # Convert minor_AF to ALT/effect allele frequency
      if (alt == minor_allele) {
        eaf = minor_AF
      } else if (ref == minor_allele) {
        eaf = 1 - minor_AF
      } else {
        eaf = "NA"
      }

      print chr, pos, variant, a1, a2, eaf, beta, se, p, n
    }
  ' >> "$output"

done
```

```{bash}
# Define header
cd ~/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab

for gwas in $(echo 50_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 21001_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 4080_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 22501_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 30780_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.varorder.tsv.bgz 20160.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 1558.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz 30760_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.varorder.tsv.bgz 20127_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.tsv.bgz); do

wget https://broad-ukb-sumstats-us-east-1.s3.amazonaws.com/round2/additive-tsvs/${gwas} -O ${gwas}

done

for file in *.tsv.bgz; do
    base=$(basename "$file" .tsv.bgz)
    output="${base}.clean.tsv"

    # Skip if the output file already exists
    if [ -f "$output" ]; then
        echo "Skipping $base: $output already exists."
        continue
    fi

    echo "Processing $base (Setting ALT as A1)..."
    echo -e "CHR\tPOS\tSNP\tA1\tA2\tEAF\tBETA\tSE\tP\tN" > ${output}
    
    # Process the file
    ~/oliverpainfel/Software/pigz -dc ${base}.tsv.bgz | \
    awk 'BEGIN {FS="\t"; OFS="\t"} {
        if (NR == 1) next; # Skip header
    
        # 1. Filter: Skip low confidence
        # (Adjust $4 to $6 if your file includes the optional AC columns)
        if ($4 == "true") next;
    
        # 2. Extract CHR:POS:REF:ALT
        split($1, v, ":");
        chr = v[1]; pos = v[2]; ref = v[3]; alt = v[4];
    
        # 3. Effect Allele Logic
        a1 = alt; # ALT is the effect allele
        a2 = ref; # REF is the non-effect allele
        
        # 4. Calculate Effect Allele Frequency (EAF)
        # If ALT is the minor allele, use minor_AF, otherwise use 1 - minor_AF
        if (alt == $2) {
            eaf = $3;
        } else {
            eaf = 1 - $3;
        }
    
        # 5. Output mapping
        # variant($1), beta($8), se($9), p($11), n($5)
        print chr, pos, $1, a1, a2, eaf, $8, $9, $11, $5
    }' >> ${output}
done
```

***

### Create Yengo COJO score file

```{r}
# I manually download the supp table 10 from the Yengo paper
library(data.table)
cojo<-fread('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/opensnp_test/yengo_cojo_eur.csv')
cojo<-cojo[, c('Chr',"BP (HG19)", 'SNP', "Effect Allele (A1)", "Conditional and joint (COJO) effect size"), with = F]
names(cojo) <- c('chr_name','chr_position','rsID','effect_allele','effect_weight')
cojo$chr_position <- as.numeric(gsub(',','',cojo$chr_position))

# Infer other_allele
setwd("~/MyGit/GenoPred/pipeline")
library(GenoUtils)
library(data.table)
source('../functions/misc.R')
source_all('../functions')

ref<-read_pvar('~/MyGit/GenoPred/pipeline/resources/data/ref/ref.chr')
cojo <- merge(cojo, ref, by.x = c('chr_name','chr_position'), by.y = c('CHR','BP'))
cojo$other_allele <- NA
cojo$other_allele[cojo$effect_allele == cojo$A1] <- cojo$A2[cojo$effect_allele == cojo$A1]
cojo$other_allele[cojo$effect_allele == cojo$A2] <- cojo$A1[cojo$effect_allele == cojo$A2]
cojo<-cojo[, c('chr_name','chr_position','rsID','effect_allele','other_allele','effect_weight'), with = F]
fwrite(cojo, '/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/opensnp_test/yengo_cojo_eur.score', sep = ' ', na = 'NA', quote = F)

```

***

### Create symlinks

```{bash}
cd ~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_10715132/imputed
for chr in {1..22}; do
  ln -s opensnp_hm3.bed opensnp_hm3.chr${chr}.bed
  ln -s opensnp_hm3.bim opensnp_hm3.chr${chr}.bim
  ln -s opensnp_hm3.fam opensnp_hm3.chr${chr}.fam
done
```

***

### Create config files

```{r}
setwd('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/MyGit/GenoPred/pipeline')
library(data.table)

# Create config file
conf <- c(
  'outdir: /users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/GenoPred/zenodo_10715132',
  'config_file: misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.yaml',
  'gwas_list: misc/opensnp/gwas_list_zenodo_10715132.txt',
  'score_list: misc/opensnp/score_list_zenodo_10715132.txt',
  'target_list: misc/opensnp/target_list_zenodo_10715132.txt',
  "pgs_methods: ['ptclump','quickprs']",
  "pgs_scaling: ['discrete','continuous']",
  "target_populations: ['EUR','TRANS']",
  "restrict_to_target_variants: T"
)

dir.create('misc/opensnp/', recursive = T)
dir.create('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/GenoPred', recursive = T)
write.table(conf, 'misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.yaml', col.names = F, row.names = F, quote = F)

# Update config to use 40 ref_pcs
conf <- c(conf, "n_ref_pcs: 40")
conf[1] <- 'outdir: /users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/GenoPred/zenodo_10715132.20_ref_pcs'
conf[2] <- 'config_file: misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.20_ref_pcs.yaml'
write.table(conf, 'misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.20_ref_pcs.yaml', col.names = F, row.names = F, quote = F)

# Create target_list
target_list <- fread('example_input/target_list.txt')
target_list <- rbind(target_list,
                     data.table(name = 'opensnp',
                                path = '/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_10715132/imputed/opensnp_hm3',
                                type = 'plink1',
                                indiv_report = F))

target_list <- target_list[target_list$name == 'opensnp', ]

write.table(target_list, 'misc/opensnp/target_list_zenodo_10715132.txt', col.names = T, row.names = F, quote = F, sep = ' ')

# Create gwas_list
library(data.table)

# Create gwas_list for Height GWAS
gwas_list <- fread('example_input/gwas_list.txt')

# Define the new datasets to add
new_gwas <- list(
  data.table(
      name='yengo_eur',
      path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/opensnp_test/yengo_2022_height_eur.txt',
      label='"Yengo 2022 Height EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='ukb_height',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/mind_the_gap/HT.gw.txt.gz',
    label='"UKB Height EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_height',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4814.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling Height EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_bmi',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4816.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling BMI EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_eduyears',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4836.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling Years of schooling EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_sysbp',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4818.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling Systolic blood pressure EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_hdl',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4844.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling HDL cholesterol EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_ldl',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4846.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling LDL cholesterol EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_alc',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4834.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling Alcohol Consumption EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_neuro',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4848.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling Neuroticism EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name='within_sib_smok',
    path='/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/Howe_2022/ieu-b-4858.txt.gz',
    label='"Within-sibling Ever Smoked EUR"'),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_height",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/50_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab Height\""
  ),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_bmi",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/21001_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab BMI\""
  ),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_sysbp",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/4080_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab Systolic Blood Pressure\""
  ),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_eduyears",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/22501_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab Years of Schooling\""
  ),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_ldl",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/30780_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.varorder.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab LDL Cholesterol\""
  ),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_smok",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/20160.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab Ever Smoked\""
  ),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_alc",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/1558.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab Alcohol Consumption\""
  ),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_hdl",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/30760_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.varorder.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab HDL Cholesterol\""
  ),
  data.table(
    name = "neale_lab_neuro",
    path = "/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/neale_lab/20127_irnt.gwas.imputed_v3.both_sexes.clean.tsv",
    label = "\"Neale Lab Neuroticism\""
  )
)

# Apply common defaults and bind them
new_gwas_dt <- rbindlist(new_gwas, fill = TRUE)
new_gwas_dt[, `:=`(
  population = 'EUR',
  n = NA,
  sampling = NA,
  prevalence = NA,
  mean = NA,
  sd = NA
)]

# Combine with original list
gwas_list <- rbind(gwas_list, new_gwas_dt)

# Filter to keep only the ones we just added
gwas_list <- gwas_list[name %in% new_gwas_dt$name]

write.table(gwas_list, 'misc/opensnp/gwas_list_zenodo_10715132.txt', col.names = T, row.names = F, quote = F, sep = ' ')

###
# Create score_list
### 

score_list<-data.table(
  name = 'yengo_cojo',
  path = '/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/GWAS_sumstats/opensnp_test/yengo_cojo_eur.score',
  label = 'yengo_cojo'
)

write.table(score_list, 'misc/opensnp/score_list_zenodo_10715132.txt', col.names = T, row.names = F, quote = F, sep = ' ')

```

***

## Run pipeline

```{bash}
gp
snakemake --profile slurm --use-conda --configfile=misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.yaml output_all ldsc outlier_detection -n 

snakemake --profile slurm --use-conda --configfile=misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.20_ref_pcs.yaml output_all ldsc outlier_detection -n 

```

***

## Check height PGS

As a sanity check, test correlation between height PGS and self-reported height in EUR individuals, with and without accounting for sex.

```{r}
setwd('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/MyGit/GenoPred/pipeline/')
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(cowplot)

source('../functions/misc.R')
source_all('../functions')

# Read in pheno data
pheno <- fread('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_10715132/qc/pheno.csv')

# Read in PGS
pgs <-
  read_pgs(
    config = 'misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.yaml',
    name = 'opensnp',
    gwas = c(
      'yengo_eur',
      'yengo_cojo',
      'within_sib_height',
      'neale_lab_height'
    )
  )$opensnp

# Restrict to EUR
pheno <- pheno[pheno$FID %in% pgs$EUR$yengo_eur$ptclump$raw$FID,]

# Check mean height of men and women
mean(pheno$height_cm[pheno$sex == 1], na.rm = T)
# 180.361
mean(pheno$height_cm[pheno$sex == 2], na.rm = T)
# 165.4815

# Estimate correlation between pheno and pgs
cor <- NULL
pop_i <- 'EUR'
for(gwas_i in names(pgs[[pop_i]])){
  for(pgs_method_i in names(pgs[[pop_i]][[gwas_i]])){
    pgs_i <- pgs$EUR[[gwas_i]][[pgs_method_i]][['raw']]
    pheno_pgs<-merge(pheno, pgs_i, by = c('FID','IID'))
    
    for(model_i in names(pgs_i)[-1:-2]){
      y <- scale(pheno_pgs$height_cm)
      x <- scale(pheno_pgs[[model_i]])
      
      if(all(is.na(x))){
        next
      }
      
      coef_i <- coef(summary(mod <- lm(y ~ x)))
      
      tmp <- data.table(
        gwas = gwas_i,
        pgs_method = pgs_method_i,
        name = model_i,
        r = coef_i[2,1],
        se = coef_i[2,2],
        p = coef_i[2,4],
        n = nobs(mod))
    cor <- rbind(cor, tmp)
    }
  }
}

cor$label <- gsub('.*_0_|.*_quickprs|.*_external', '', cor$name)
cor$label[cor$label != '']<-paste0('p+t: ', cor$label[cor$label != ''])
cor$label[cor$pgs_method == 'quickprs']<-'QuickPRS'
cor$label[grepl('cojo', cor$gwas)]<-'COJO'
cor$label<-factor(cor$label, levels = unique(cor$label))

cor$gwas[grepl('cojo', cor$gwas)]<-'yengo_eur'
cor$gwas <- factor(cor$gwas, 
                   levels = c("yengo_eur","neale_lab_height","within_sib_height"),
                   labels = c('Yengo','Neale Lab','Howe Within-sib'))

# Plot results
ggplot(cor, aes(x = label, y = r)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = r - se, ymax = r + se), width = 0.2) +  
  facet_grid(gwas ~ .) +
  theme_half_open() +
  panel_border() +
  background_grid() +
  labs(x = 'PGS Method', y = 'Pearson Correlation') +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1))

###
# Check correlation with height PGS from Frei et al.
###
frei_pgs <- fread('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/zenodo_10715132/pgs/height_cm.all_score')
pheno_pgs <- merge(pheno, frei_pgs, by = c('FID','IID'))
cor_frei <- NULL
for(model_i in names(frei_pgs)[-1:-2]){
  y <- scale(pheno_pgs$height_cm)
  x <- scale(pheno_pgs[[model_i]])
  
  if(all(is.na(x))){
    next
  }
  
  coef_i <- coef(summary(mod <- lm(y ~ x)))
  
  tmp <- data.table(
    name = model_i,
    r = coef_i[2,1],
    se = coef_i[2,2],
    p = coef_i[2,4],
    n = nobs(mod))
  cor_frei <- rbind(cor_frei, tmp)
}

cor_frei$label <- paste0('p+t: ', gsub('Pt_', '', cor_frei$name))
cor_frei$label <- gsub('0.0001', '1e-04', cor_frei$label)
cor_frei$type = 'Frei'

cor_neale<-cor[cor$gwas == 'Neale Lab' & cor$pgs_method == 'ptclump',]
cor_neale$gwas <- NULL
cor_neale$pgs_method <- NULL
cor_neale$type = 'GenoPred'
cor_neale<-cor_neale[cor_neale$label %in% cor_frei$label,]
cor_frei<-cor_frei[cor_frei$label %in% cor_neale$label,]

cor_both <- rbind(cor_frei, cor_neale)

ggplot(cor_both, aes(x = label, y = r)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = r - se, ymax = r + se), width = 0.2) +  
  facet_grid( ~ type) +
  theme_half_open() +
  panel_border() +
  background_grid() +
  labs(x = 'PGS Method', y = 'Pearson Correlation') +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1))


####
# Test correlation with height adjusted for sex
####

# Estimate correlation between pheno and pgs
cor_resid <- NULL
for(gwas_i in names(pgs[[pop_i]])){
  for(pgs_method_i in names(pgs[[pop_i]][[gwas_i]])){
    pgs_i <- pgs$EUR[[gwas_i]][[pgs_method_i]][['raw']]
    pheno_pgs<-merge(pheno, pgs_i, by = c('FID','IID'))
    pheno_pgs<-pheno_pgs[!is.na(pheno_pgs$height_cm),]
    
    for(model_i in names(pgs_i)[-1:-2]){
      y <- scale(resid(lm(pheno_pgs$height_cm ~ pheno_pgs$sex)))
      x <- scale(pheno_pgs[[model_i]])
      
      if(all(is.na(x))){
        next
      }
      
      coef_i <- coef(summary(mod <- lm(y ~ x)))
      
      tmp <- data.table(
        gwas = gwas_i,
        pgs_method = pgs_method_i,
        name = model_i,
        r = coef_i[2,1],
        se = coef_i[2,2],
        p = coef_i[2,4],
        n = nobs(mod))
    cor_resid <- rbind(cor_resid, tmp)
    }
  }
}

cor_resid$label <- gsub('.*_0_|.*_quickprs|.*_external', '', cor_resid$name)
cor_resid$label[cor_resid$label != '']<-paste0('p+t: ', cor_resid$label[cor_resid$label != ''])
cor_resid$label[cor_resid$pgs_method == 'quickprs']<-'QuickPRS'
cor_resid$label[grepl('cojo', cor_resid$gwas)]<-'COJO'
cor_resid$label<-factor(cor_resid$label, levels = unique(cor_resid$label))

cor_resid$gwas[grepl('cojo', cor_resid$gwas)]<-'yengo_eur'
cor_resid$gwas <- factor(cor_resid$gwas, 
                   levels = c("yengo_eur","neale_lab_height","within_sib_height"),
                   labels = c('Yengo','Neale Lab','Howe Within-sib'))


# Plot Yengo results with and without sex correction
cor_yengo <- cor[cor$gwas == 'Yengo',]
cor_yengo$Type <- 'Raw Height'
cor_resid_yengo <- cor_resid[cor_resid$gwas == 'Yengo',]
cor_resid_yengo$Type <- 'Sex-Corrected Height'

cor_yengo_both <- rbind(cor_yengo, cor_resid_yengo)

ggplot(cor_yengo_both, aes(x = label, y = r)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = r - se, ymax = r + se), width = 0.2) +  
  facet_grid(. ~ Type) +
  theme_half_open() +
  panel_border() +
  background_grid() +
  labs(x = 'PGS Method', y = 'Pearson Correlation') +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1))

# Make a table listing the number of variants in each PGS
score_file_list <- list_score_files('misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.yaml')
score_file_list <- score_file_list[grepl('height|yengo', score_file_list$name),]

n_snp <- NULL
for(i in 1:nrow(score_file_list)){
  tmp <- fread(paste0('~/oliverpainfel/Data/OpenSNP/GenoPred/zenodo_10715132/reference/pgs_score_files/',score_file_list$method[i],'/',score_file_list$name[i],'/ref-',score_file_list$name[i],'.score.gz'))
  n_snp_i <- apply(tmp[,-1:-3, with = F], 2, function(x) sum(x != 0))
  n_snp <- rbind(
    n_snp,
    data.table(
      name = score_file_list$name[i],
      method = score_file_list$method[i],
      param = names(n_snp_i),
      n_snp = n_snp_i
    )
  )
}

n_snp[grepl('5e-08|quickprs|external', param),]

```

***

## Check mean and sd of PGS

```{r}
setwd('/users/k1806347/oliverpainfel/Software/MyGit/GenoPred/pipeline/')
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(cowplot)

source('../functions/misc.R')
source_all('../functions')

# Set config
config <- 'misc/opensnp/config_zenodo_10715132.yaml'
gwas_list <- fread('misc/opensnp/gwas_list_zenodo_10715132.txt')

# Target OpenSNP PGS
target_pgs <- read_pgs(
  config = config,
  name = "opensnp"
)

# Print mean PGS using GenoPred corrections
pgs_desc <- NULL
for(i in names(target_pgs$opensnp)){
  for(j in names(target_pgs$opensnp[[i]])){
    for(k in names(target_pgs$opensnp[[i]][[j]])){
      for(m in names(target_pgs$opensnp[[i]][[j]][[k]])){
        for(n in names(target_pgs$opensnp[[i]][[j]][[k]][[m]])[-1:-2]){
          pgs_desc <- rbind(
            pgs_desc,
            data.table(
              pop = i,
              gwas = j,
              method = k,
              type = m,
              param = n,
              mean = mean(target_pgs$opensnp[[i]][[j]][[k]][[m]][[n]]),
              sd = sd(target_pgs$opensnp[[i]][[j]][[k]][[m]][[n]])
            ))
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

# Plot height results
pgs_desc_height <- pgs_desc[pgs_desc$gwas %in% c('yengo_eur','neale_lab_height','within_sib_height','yengo_cojo'),]

library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)

pgs_desc_height$label <- gsub('.*_0_|.*_quickprs|.*_external', '', pgs_desc_height$param)
pgs_desc_height$label[pgs_desc_height$label != '']<-paste0('p+t: ', pgs_desc_height$label[pgs_desc_height$label != ''])
pgs_desc_height$label[pgs_desc_height$method == 'quickprs']<-'QuickPRS'
pgs_desc_height$label[grepl('cojo', pgs_desc_height$gwas)]<-'COJO'
pgs_desc_height$label<-factor(pgs_desc_height$label, levels = unique(pgs_desc_height$label))

pgs_desc_height$gwas[grepl('cojo', pgs_desc_height$gwas)]<-'yengo_eur'
pgs_desc_height$gwas <- factor(pgs_desc_height$gwas, 
                   levels = c("yengo_eur","neale_lab_height","within_sib_height"),
                   labels = c('Yengo','Neale Lab','Howe Within-sib'))

# Retain p+t with 5e-8 only
```
